##### II.2.a.b — AI does not expect: the mistake

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META — placement, evidence, red-team. This is the most contestable pin in the gallery.**
- The node: the system has an internal model but its own chosen action does not flag as catastrophic — competent pursuit of a misspecified goal, not a deliberate betrayal. Cleanest fit is proxy-drift / "you get what you measure" / specification-gaming, and Christiano's "What Failure Looks Like, Part I."
- **Why Christiano lands here, not with Yudkowsky:** his distinctive beliefs are tractability (Crux D — oversight is partly workable, failure is path-dependent) and that current behavior is evidence (Crux C). Empirically-conditioned, so he expects a messy, partly-catchable loss of control rather than a single brittle treacherous turn. Evidence: "My views on 'doom'" (22% = 15% pre-obsolescence + 7% later); "What Failure Looks Like" (2019).
- **Red-team:** (1) Christiano's Part II ("going out with a bang") is a takeover — that's the *expects* leaf, so he straddles a.a/a.b; we anchor him here via the proxy-drift reading. (2) *Sleeper Agents* is trained *deception* — an *expects* phenomenon — so it supports the broad "alignment methods are unreliable" claim but not this node specifically; a spec-gaming example would fit better. (3) For the systematic pass: should Control Failure sit at a.a (the optimistic-on-tractability cousin of the Sharp Left Turn) rather than a.b?
- **Amodei is placed here by mechanism (Control Failure); not a straddle.** His race-logic is level-3 decision-context (why build), not a second branch. Evidence: Axios (2025); "Machines of Loving Grace" (2024).
:::

Here the system understands catastrophe in the abstract, but its own chosen action does not flag as one. It is not a betrayal; it is competent pursuit of the goal we actually gave it, whose pursuit turns out to be catastrophic, and we lose control without anyone, human or machine, intending it. Post 2's cases are concrete: an action safe in isolation that triggers a butterfly effect past the model's horizon, or one safe in training that turns fatal out of distribution.

**Control Failure.** The risk is high, but the failure is less brittle than a treacherous turn. The threat is the messy, likely reality that institutions cannot reliably align advanced capability before deploying it — Anthropic's *Sleeper Agents* showed [standard safety training failing to remove hidden misbehavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05566), evidence that our alignment methods are not yet trustworthy. Paul Christiano puts [irreversible takeover near 22 percent](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/xWMqsvHapP3nwdSW8/my-views-on-doom), and Dario Amodei [around 25 percent that things go "really, really badly"](https://www.axios.com/2025/09/17/anthropic-dario-amodei-p-doom-25-percent); both hold it. What separates them from the Sharp Left Turn is not the mechanism but two cruxes: they think oversight is partly tractable and that today's systems are real evidence, so they expect a path-dependent, sometimes-catchable loss of control rather than a single unrecoverable gate. Christiano is the gallery's one genuine straddle, and it's worth admitting: his sharper scenarios edge up toward the knowing optimizer above, and what holds him on this branch is less the mechanism than the temperament, a bet that failure comes gradually and partly catchably rather than as one locked door. Amodei, by contrast, sits here cleanly by mechanism; his lab-building logic — better that a safety-focused lab leads — is a decision about what to do given the race, not a second threat model. What moves them: interpretability or scalable alignment lowers their estimates; another failure to train out hidden misbehavior raises them.

::: {.aside}
The misuse route has no clean home. Control Failure also carries deliberate misuse of an aligned-but-compliant system, a state synthesizing a pathogen, but there the system has no misaligned model and the agent who expects the catastrophe is the human. The tree sorts machine cognition, not human intent, so misuse sits awkwardly across it.
:::
